surviving.title: Resume after ad-creation failure orphans the previously-created Meta creative and re-uploads the image
surviving.evidence: Ad-loop resume guard only checks meta_ad_id: `if (ad.meta_ad_id) { metaAdIdsToActivate.add(ad.meta_ad_id); continue; }`. The creative id is persisted separately at line 1037 (`.from('ads').update({ meta_creative_id: creative.id })`) but the ad select at line 748 does NOT fetch `meta_creative_id` (columns: `id, meta_ad_id, name, headline, primary_text, description, cta, media_asset_id, angle, creative_format, creative_variant_key, utm_content_key`), and there is no `meta_creative_id` guard anywhere in the ad block.
surviving.failureScenario: createMetaAdCreative succeeds (meta_creative_id saved) but createMetaAd (line 1041) fails -> catch rolls back by pausing objects, resets to DRAFT. User clicks Retry: ad.meta_ad_id is still null, so the whole block re-runs — it re-signs the asset, re-uploads the image to Meta (new adimage), and calls createMetaAdCreative again, creating a SECOND creative. The first creative is never deleted (rollback only pauses; creatives have no status). Every retry leaks another orphaned creative + adimage, and meta_creative_id is overwritten so the orphan is unrecoverable.
surviving.rootCause: Idempotency guard is keyed only on meta_ad_id; the intermediate meta_creative_id checkpoint that is written to the DB is neither selected back nor used to skip creative re-creation on resume.
surviving.fix: Add `meta_creative_id` to the ads select at line 748 and to AdRow, then before uploading/creating a creative reuse it when present (skip uploadMetaImage + createMetaAdCreative if ad.meta_creative_id is set, and go straight to createMetaAd with the stored creative id). This makes creative creation idempotent on retry.
surviving.siblingCheck: Checked pauseMetaObject/setMetaObjectStatus in src/lib/meta/marketing.ts (creatives have no ACTIVE/PAUSED lifecycle so rollback cannot remove them); checked the ad-set resume guard (line 911, correctly keyed on meta_adset_id) and campaign resume guard (line 846, keyed on meta_campaign_id) — only the creative checkpoint is missing a guard.
surviving.verifyReasoning: Every load-bearing fact in the claim is verified against the code in src/app/(app)/campaigns/[id]/actions.ts. (1) The ads select at line 748 does NOT fetch meta_creative_id, and the AdRow interface (lines 67-80) has no meta_creative_id field. (2) The resume guard for ads at line 971 keys solely on ad.meta_ad_id (`if (ad.meta_ad_id) { metaAdIdsToActivate.add(...); continue; }`); there is no meta_creative_id guard anywhere in the ad block. (3) meta_creative_id is written at line 1037 but grep confirms that line is the ONLY reference in the file — it is never read back. (4) createMetaAdCreative (line 1017) runs and persists the creative id BEFORE createMetaAd (line 1041); a failure at 1041 throws with the creative already created and its id saved to the DB. (5) Rollback (lines 1132-1140) only calls pauseMetaObject, which POSTs status:PAUSED (marketing.ts lines 489-506); ad creatives have no ACTIVE/PAUSED lifecycle, so the pause errors and is swallowed — the creative is never deleted. (6) createdMetaObjects is function-scoped (line 660), so on a retry the earlier orphan is not tracked and cannot be cleaned up. (7) Sibling guards are correctly keyed: ad-set at line 911 (meta_adset_id) and campaign at line 846 (meta_campaign_id); only the creative checkpoint lacks a guard. Therefore the failure scenario is fully reproducible: when createMetaAd fails after createMetaAdCreative succeeds, a retry re-signs the asset, re-uploads the image (new adimage), and creates a SECOND creative, overwriting meta_creative_id and orphaning the first creative+adimage unrecoverably, once per retry. Severity high is justified because the leak is silent, unbounded per retry, and unrecoverable; the only mitigating factor (publish eventually succeeds, so it degrades gracefully) argues for medium at worst, but the data-hygiene/resource-leak risk supports the high rating.

surviving.title: Food ad-set spend caps use raw un-normalised template weights, so enabling FOOD_OPTIMISATION hard-fails every multi-service food publish/materialise
surviving.evidence: computeAdSetSpendCaps: `const target = (weight / 100) * campaignBudget;` with doc `target = (weight / 100) × campaignBudget` expecting weights that 'sum to ~100'. But the weights passed in are the raw per-service template weights: createFoodBookingCampaign persists `budget_weight: window.budgetWeight` (=template `t.weight`) at actions.ts:1262; publish reads `budgetWeight: adSet.budget_weight` ([id]/actions.ts:829); materialise passes `budgetWeight: window.budgetWeight` (food-materialise.ts:615). computeFoodWindowWeights (the only function that normalises weights to sum ~100 and applies servicePriority/dayWeighting/bookingGap) has ZERO production callers.
surviving.failureScenario: A food_booking campaign with all three default services enabled produces enabled-window weights summing to ~280 (weekday 55+35=90, saturday 25+35+30=90, sunday 20+35+30+15=100). With FOOD_OPTIMISATION_ENABLED=true and a £100 budget, computeAdSetSpendCaps computes each minBudget=max(1, target*0.5); the floored minimums sum to ~£140, which exceeds £100, so the preflight returns an error and publishCampaign (setPublishError, returns {error}) / materialiseFoodWindowsForCampaign (throws) aborts. Every realistic multi-service food campaign fails to publish or roll over the moment the optimisation flag is enabled.
surviving.rootCause: budget_weight is stored as the raw decision-stage template weight (a per-service local number that does not sum to 100 across the campaign), but computeAdSetSpendCaps treats it as a normalised percentage of the whole campaign budget. The normalising step (computeFoodWindowWeights, which would also apply the user's dayWeighting/manualDayWeights and servicePriority) is never wired into the create/publish/materialise paths.
surviving.fix: Before persisting, call computeFoodWindowWeights({ windows: enabledWindows, dayWeighting: brief.dayWeighting, manualDayWeights: brief.manualDayWeights }) and write the normalised (sum≈100) weight to ad_sets.budget_weight, instead of window.budgetWeight (the raw template weight). Then spend-cap maths, dayWeighting and manualDayWeights all take effect. Add a unit test asserting persisted budget_weights sum to ~100 and that computeAdSetSpendCaps' minSum ≤ budget for a normal 3-service campaign.
surviving.verifyReasoning:
Every link in the claim's chain verified by reading source. (1) computeAdSetSpendCaps (food-budget-weighting.ts:187) computes target=(weight/100)*campaignBudget and its docstring explicitly expects weights that "sum to ~100"; preflight (lines 199-207) returns an error when the floored minimums exceed the campaign budget. (2) The weights actually fed in are raw per-service template weights, never normalised: food-booking-phases.ts:86 sets budgetWeight: t.weight; create persists budget_weight: window.budgetWeight with no normalisation between (actions.ts:1129 is a plain filter, :1262 the insert); publish reads adSet.budget_weight ([id]/actions.ts:829); materialise passes window.budgetWeight (food-materialise.ts:615). (3) The only normaliser, computeFoodWindowWeights (which alone divides by the total to sum ~100 and applies servicePriority/dayNeed/bookingGap), has ZERO callers anywhere — grep returns only its own definition. So dayWeighting, manualDayWeights and servicePriority are all dead too. (4) Arithmetic confirmed by direct computation: a 3-service default campaign's default-enabled windows carry weights [55,35 / 25,35,30 / 20,35,30,15] summing to 280 (not 100); with a £100 budget, minSum=max(1, weight*0.5) summed = £140 > £100, so preflight returns the error. (5) Both consuming paths abort on that error: publish calls setPublishError and returns {error} ([id]/actions.ts:833-835); materialise throws (food-materialise.ts:366). 

Refutation angles considered and rejected: The FOOD_OPTIMISATION_ENABLED flag defaults off (env.ts:188-192), so the bug is latent — but the claim's title and scenario both explicitly condition on the flag being enabled, so this is correct scoping, not overstatement; the feature is otherwise fully wired and breaks the moment it is switched on. The word "every" is very slightly strong because a large enough budget could clear preflight, and materialise operates only on per-rollover newWindows (smaller minSum) rather than the full set — but the publish path uses the whole campaign ad-set set, and for realistic pub budgets (£10-50) the failure is effectively guaranteed. More fundamentally, even where preflight happens to pass, the cap model is systematically mis-scaled: weights meant to sum ~100 actually sum ~280, so every target/min/max is ~2.8x too large and demand-proportioning is wrong. This is a genuine, high-severity defect that silently blocks the entire Phase 3 optimisation feature from ever functioning correctly. High severity is justified.

surviving.title: Dashboard Sync / Optimise / Approve buttons swallow server-action errors — no toast or inline feedback
surviving.evidence: CommandCentre renders `<form action={syncPerformanceFormAction}>` (line 202) and `<form action={runOptimiserFormAction}>` (line 207); PrimaryActionButton does the same (lines 220, 230); RecommendationPreview renders `<form action={applyOptimisationRecommendationFormAction}>` (line 1155). These wrappers (actions.ts:1940-1953) call functions that RETURN error objects and discard them: `syncPerformanceFormAction` -> `syncCampaignDashboardPerformance()` which returns `{ error: 'N campaigns failed to sync.' }` (actions.ts:1568), `runOptimiserFormAction` -> returns `{ error }` on catch, and `applyOptimisationRecommendationFormAction` -> `applyOptimisationRecommendation()` which returns `{ error: 'This recommendation has already been handled.' }` etc. None of these return values are read, so no toast/inline error is shown.
surviving.failureScenario: A user's Meta token has expired. They click 'Sync' (or the primary 'Sync performance' action). `syncCampaignDashboardPerformance` catches every per-campaign failure and returns `{ error: '3 campaigns failed to sync', synced: 0, failed: 3 }`. The form action discards this; the page silently revalidates with stale metrics and no error. The user believes the data is fresh and makes budget decisions on stale numbers. Likewise, clicking 'Approve replacement' on a recommendation already handled in another tab returns `{ error: 'This recommendation has already been handled.' }` and appears to do nothing, with no explanation.
surviving.rootCause: Server actions passed to `<form action={}>` cannot return values to the client, and the wrapper functions were written to fire-and-forget. Unlike CampaignActions.tsx (which awaits results and surfaces `toast.error(...)`), the dashboard-level form-action buttons have no result channel and no error UI.
surviving.siblingCheck: Compared against CampaignActions.tsx (lines 33-36, 69-71, 104-105) and RecurringControls (lines 44-48) which DO surface errors via toast; the dashboard CommandCentre/PrimaryActionButton/RecommendationPreview forms and the detail page's applyOptimisationRecommendationFormAction (page.tsx:297-303, 333-348) are the ones that swallow errors. Confirmed the underlying actions return `{ error }` rather than throwing (actions.ts:1550, 1568, 1670-1674).
surviving.fix: Convert these buttons to client-side handlers using `useTransition` + `useToast` (mirroring CampaignActions.tsx): call the value-returning server actions directly, check for `error` in the result, and show `toast.error(...)`. At minimum, surface the returned error string. Keep the disabled/pending state during the transition to also fix double-submit.
surviving.verifyReasoning: Verified by direct inspection of both cited files. In CampaignDashboard.tsx the CommandCentre renders `<form action={syncPerformanceFormAction}>` (line 202) and `<form action={runOptimiserFormAction}>` (line 207); PrimaryActionButton renders the same wrappers (lines 220, 230); RecommendationPreview renders `<form action={applyOptimisationRecommendationFormAction}>` (line 1155) with a hidden actionId input. In actions.ts (lines 1940-1953) all three wrapper functions `await` value-returning underlying actions and discard the result — `syncPerformanceFormAction` calls `syncCampaignDashboardPerformance()` (which returns `{ error: 'N campaign(s) failed to sync.', synced, failed }` at line 1570 after catching every per-campaign failure), `runOptimiserFormAction` calls `runCampaignDashboardOptimisation()` (returns `{ error }` on catch at line 1603), and `applyOptimisationRecommendationFormAction` calls `applyOptimisationRecommendation()` (returns `{ error }`). Because Next.js `<form action={serverAction}>` cannot pipe a return value back to the client and these wrappers return void, there is no toast/inline error channel. The sibling CampaignActions.tsx (lines 29-41, 63-75, 99-112) proves the correct pattern is available and used elsewhere: it uses useTransition + useToast, awaits the result, checks for `error`, and calls `toast.error(...)`. The failure scenario is sound: an expired Meta token yields per-campaign sync failures that are caught, counted, and returned as `{ error }`, which the wrapper silently drops; the page revalidates with stale metrics and no user feedback. Likewise an already-handled recommendation returns `{ error }` and the Approve button appears to do nothing. This is a real UX gap (silent server-action error swallowing), not a misread. Severity 'high' is at the upper boundary for a pure feedback gap (the sync itself still executes; only error surfacing is missing), but is defensible given the dashboard drives budget decisions on the displayed numbers and offers zero error indication.

surviving.title: RecurringControls (pause/resume/stop) never renders on the campaign detail page — guard checks impossible campaignKind values
surviving.evidence: `{['weekly', 'weekly_recurring', 'daily', 'monthly'].includes(campaign.campaignKind) && (<RecurringControls ... />)}`. But `campaign` comes from `getCampaignWithTree` -> `dbRowToCampaign` which sets `campaignKind: (row.campaign_kind ?? 'event') as PaidCampaignKind` (actions.ts:2176), and `PaidCampaignKind = 'event' | 'evergreen' | 'food_booking'` (types/campaigns.ts:14). None of those equal 'weekly'/'weekly_recurring'/'daily'/'monthly', so the condition is always false.
surviving.failureScenario: An owner opens a recurring campaign's detail page expecting Pause / Resume / Stop controls (the feature documented in recurring-controls.tsx header '06-05, D-14'). The guard on line 105 evaluates false for every meta_campaigns row, so RecurringControls is never mounted. The pause/resume/stop lifecycle is completely unreachable from the UI.
surviving.rootCause: Two different status/kind vocabularies were conflated. The 'weekly'/'daily'/'monthly' values belong to the separate `campaigns` table's `campaign_type` (see src/app/actions/content.ts:734 mapping weekly_recurring->'weekly'), but the campaigns detail page is backed by `meta_campaigns`, whose `campaignKind` is always event/evergreen/food_booking. RecurringControls is only imported here (grep confirms single usage), so it is effectively dead code.
surviving.siblingCheck: Grepped all usages of RecurringControls and 'weekly_recurring' across src/: only import site is page.tsx:15/106. Confirmed getCampaignWithTree reads from `meta_campaigns` (actions.ts:1369) and the mapper never yields recurring kinds.
surviving.fix: Decide the intended data source. If recurring lifecycle applies to meta_campaigns, gate on the real field (e.g. a recurring flag or campaignType) that meta_campaigns actually carries, and pass a status the component understands. If it applies to the `campaigns` table, this component/wiring belongs on a different page. Do not ship the current always-false guard.
surviving.verifyReasoning: Verified against source. page.tsx:105 gates RecurringControls on ['weekly','weekly_recurring','daily','monthly'].includes(campaign.campaignKind). But campaignKind comes from dbRowToCampaign (actions.ts:2177) as (row.campaign_kind ?? 'event') as PaidCampaignKind, and PaidCampaignKind = 'event' | 'evergreen' | 'food_booking' (types/campaigns.ts:14). getCampaignWithTree reads from meta_campaigns (actions.ts:1369); the only three INSERT sites set campaign_kind to 'evergreen', 'food_booking', or meta.campaignKind (actions.ts:502/818/1228). A grep for campaign_kind assigned any of weekly/daily/monthly/recurring returned nothing. Therefore campaignKind can only ever be one of event/evergreen/food_booking — none of which is in the guard array — so the condition is always false and RecurringControls (imported/used only here, page.tsx:15/106) never mounts. The pause/resume/stop lifecycle is unreachable from the campaign detail page. The root-cause analysis is also correct: weekly/promotion are campaign_type values of the separate `campaigns` table (content.ts:734, materialise.ts:30, scheduling/campaign-timing.ts:39), a different vocabulary from meta_campaigns.campaignKind. An additional mismatch reinforces the defect: even if the guard fired, RecurringControls' internal status checks expect 'scheduled'/'paused'/'completed' (recurring-controls.tsx:121-162) while meta_campaigns uses DRAFT/ACTIVE/PAUSED/ARCHIVED, so the buttons still would not render — confirming the wiring targets the wrong table end to end. High severity stands: a documented feature (06-05, D-14) is entirely dead in the paid-campaigns UI. This is a genuine, reproducible always-false guard, not a misread or a case the code handles elsewhere.

surviving.title: RecurringControls never renders: detail page gates on campaignKind, which can only be event/evergreen/food_booking
surviving.failureScenario: campaign.campaignKind is derived in dbRowToCampaign (actions.ts:2177: `(row.campaign_kind ?? 'event')`) from meta_campaigns.campaign_kind, whose DB check constraint only permits event|evergreen (food_booking added later) — never weekly/daily/monthly. The includes() test is always false, so the pause/resume/stop lifecycle controls (feature 06-05, D-14) never render for any campaign on this page.
surviving.rootCause: The gate should test the recurring cadence field, not campaignKind. Recurring cadence lives in the organic `campaigns` table's `campaign_type` column ('weekly' etc.), but this page reads the `meta_campaigns` (paid Meta ads) table which has no such column. campaign.campaignType also collapses to campaign_kind here (meta_campaigns has no campaign_type column), so switching the gate to campaignType would not fix it.
surviving.fix: RecurringControls belongs to the organic `campaigns` lifecycle, not the paid meta_campaigns detail page. Either remove this dead block, or if recurring paid campaigns are intended, add campaign_type/auto_confirm columns to meta_campaigns, populate + select them, and gate on the cadence field. Do not gate on campaignKind.
surviving.siblingCheck: Grepped all usages of RecurringControls/pauseRecurringCampaign across src: only page.tsx wires the component and only app/actions/campaigns.ts implements the actions. recurring-controls.tsx has its own duplicate isRecurring gate (line 35) on the campaignType prop with the same value problem.
surviving.verifyReasoning:
Verified against source and live schema. The detail page (src/app/(app)/campaigns/[id]/page.tsx:105) gates RecurringControls on `['weekly','weekly_recurring','daily','monthly'].includes(campaign.campaignKind)`. `campaign.campaignKind` is populated by dbRowToCampaign (actions.ts:2177) as `(row.campaign_kind ?? 'event')` and typed `PaidCampaignKind = 'event' | 'evergreen' | 'food_booking'` (src/types/campaigns.ts:14). The page's data source is `getCampaignWithTree`, which queries `.from('meta_campaigns')` (actions.ts:1369). The DB check constraint on meta_campaigns.campaign_kind is `CHECK (campaign_kind = ANY (ARRAY['event','evergreen']))` (supabase/baseline/v1_baseline.sql:345), with food_booking added later (migration 20260608115311). So campaignKind can never be weekly/weekly_recurring/daily/monthly, and the includes() test is unconditionally false — RecurringControls (feature 06-05, D-14 pause/resume/stop lifecycle) never renders on this page.

The campaignType fallback does not rescue it: SCHEMA.md confirms meta_campaigns has NO campaign_type column (the campaign_type/auto_confirm columns at SCHEMA.md:201/205 belong to the separate organic `campaigns` table, not meta_campaigns). Thus row.campaign_type is undefined and dbRowToCampaign (actions.ts:2190) collapses campaignType to campaign_kind. The prop `campaign.campaignType ?? campaign.campaignKind` is therefore always one of the three paid kinds, and recurring-controls.tsx's own isRecurring gate (line 34) is likewise always false. The CampaignDbRow interface declaring campaign_type/auto_confirm (actions.ts:40-41) is simply inaccurate for the meta_campaigns SELECT and yields undefined at runtime.

Severity high is justified: an entire documented feature is dead for every campaign on this page. Not critical (no data loss/security impact) — it is dead/unreachable UI — but the claim scopes it correctly.

surviving.title: Optimiser can recommend pausing an ad that drove management-sourced bookings (utm_content null)
surviving.evidence: function adHasTrackedBooking(ad, bookingSignal) { return metric(ad.metrics_conversions) > 0 || (bookingSignal?.adBookings[ad.id] ?? 0) > 0; }  // adBookings only populated when findAttributedAd matches event.utm_content
surviving.failureScenario: loadManagementBookingConversionEvents (optimisation.ts:983) returns events with utm_content: null. buildBlendedBookingSignals -> findAttributedAd (line 460/1063) returns null for every null-utm_content event, so those bookings never land in adBookings[ad.id]; they only raise campaign-level firstPartyBookings. If ad A has a Meta Purchase conversion (so adsWithBookings.length>0 branch fires, line 398) and sibling ad B actually drove the management-sourced bookings but has 0 Meta conversions and no utm_content match, ad B has adHasTrackedBooking=false and, once its spend>=12 or clicks>=30, is emitted as a pause_ad recommendation despite having produced real bookings.
surviving.rootCause: Ad-level booking attribution depends solely on utm_content, but the management booking fallback path cannot supply utm_content, so ad-level first-party bookings are silently dropped while campaign-level counts still include them — the ad-level 'has a tracked booking' test is blind to that source.
surviving.siblingCheck: Checked evaluateAdSetOptimisation low-CTR branch (line 415) and evaluateCopyRewriteRecommendations (line 648, guarded by campaign-level blendedBookings) — copy-rewrite is protected by blended campaign bookings, but the sibling-pause branch relies on per-ad attribution only.
surviving.fix: When a campaign has management-fallback first-party bookings that cannot be attributed to a specific ad (utm_content null), suppress the sibling-pause branch for that ad set (e.g. treat all ads as potentially booking-bearing), or record unattributable first-party bookings at ad-set level and factor them into adHasTrackedBooking. At minimum, do not pause an ad in an ad set whose parent campaign has unattributed first-party bookings.
surviving.verifyReasoning:
Traced the full chain in src/lib/campaigns/optimisation.ts and every link holds.

1. loadManagementBookingConversionEvents (line 926) unconditionally sets utm_content: null on every emitted event (line 984).
2. These management events are always concatenated with first-party events and passed to buildBlendedBookingSignals (lines 856-857) — it is an additive source, not a mutually-exclusive fallback, so the co-occurrence the scenario needs is normal, not contrived.
3. bookingEventMatchesCampaign (lines 1085-1090) matches on event_id / event_slug / short_code, independent of utm_content. So a null-utm_content management booking DOES match a campaign and increments matchedEvents -> firstPartyBookings -> blendedBookings (lines 469, 476).
4. But inside the same loop, findAttributedAd(campaign, event.utm_content) returns null for null utm_content (line 1064), hitting `continue` at line 461, so the booking is never recorded in adBookings[ad.id].
5. adHasTrackedBooking (line 1060) only checks ad.metrics_conversions (Meta) and adBookings[ad.id]; it has no visibility of campaign-level first-party bookings.
6. Sibling-pause branch (lines 398-413): if any ad A has a Meta conversion, adsWithBookings.length > 0 fires; a sibling ad B with 0 Meta conversions and no ad-level attribution is emitted as a pause recommendation once metrics_spend >= 12 or metrics_clicks >= 30 (constants confirmed at lines 31-32). If ad B is in fact the ad that drove the management-sourced (utm_content-null) booking, it is recommended for pause despite having produced a real booking.

The defect is genuine: ad-level booking attribution depends solely on utm_content, but the management-fallback path structurally cannot supply utm_content, so those bookings are silently dropped at ad level while still inflating campaign-level counts, and the sibling-pause test is blind to that source.

Severity assessment (guarding against inflation): the output is an advisory 'Recommended pause' decision recorded as planned (statuses planned/applied, lines 1006-1008; reason strings say 'Recommended pause'), not an auto-applied pause, and the trigger set is narrow (management connection enabled; a booking whose only signal is the null-utm_content management row; ad A carries a Meta conversion; ad B crosses spend/click thresholds with zero Meta conversions of its own — in practice Meta's own pixel would often also credit ad B, shrinking the true blind spot). That justifies the low confidence and keeps this at medium rather than high — a wrong recommendation on a revenue-driving ad is materially bad but reversible and human-gated. Confirmed at medium.

surviving.title: Booking-conversion ingest secret uses non-timing-safe string comparison
surviving.failureScenario: An attacker probing the public /api/booking-conversions endpoint can use response-time differences from the short-circuiting `!==` comparison to recover BOOKING_CONVERSION_INGEST_SECRET byte-by-byte, then forge arbitrary booking conversions (inflating campaign bookings/value on the dashboard, and pushing forged Purchase events into Meta CAPI). The sibling cron route deliberately uses a constant-time compare (verifyCronAuth -> validateSecret in src/lib/security/cron-auth.ts), so this endpoint is inconsistent and weaker.
surviving.rootCause: Raw `!==` comparison of the supplied secret against the configured secret instead of the project's constant-time `validateSecret` helper used everywhere else for shared-secret auth.
surviving.siblingCheck: Compared against src/lib/security/cron-auth.ts (retry-capi cron) which uses validateSecret() from src/lib/security/signing.ts; the ingest route is the only shared-secret check here that does a plain string compare.
surviving.fix: Replace `suppliedSecret !== secret` with the constant-time helper, e.g. `import { validateSecret } from '@/lib/security/signing'` and `if (!validateSecret(suppliedSecret, secret)) return jsonNoStore({ error: 'Unauthorized' }, { status: 401 });`.
surviving.verifyReasoning: Verified against source. src/app/api/booking-conversions/route.ts:105-108 authenticates a public ingest endpoint with a plain `suppliedSecret !== secret` string comparison, which is non-constant-time and short-circuits. The project ships a timing-safe helper `validateSecret()` (crypto.timingSafeEqual) in src/lib/security/signing.ts explicitly for webhook/cron secrets (referencing AUTH-06), and the sibling cron route src/lib/security/cron-auth.ts:53 uses it for the identical shared-secret pattern. So the finding's core facts all hold: this is a real deviation from the project's own established constant-time-compare convention on a security-sensitive auth check, and the suggested fix (swap in validateSecret) is trivial and already available. I confirm rather than reject because the defect exists regardless of exploit practicality. On severity: the failure narrative (recover the secret byte-by-byte via remote timing to forge CAPI/dashboard bookings) overstates real-world exploitability — remote timing attacks against a single JS string compare are dominated by network jitter and V8 does not guarantee predictable per-byte timing — but the endpoint is genuinely public and guards forgeable Meta CAPI/dashboard data, and it violates the codebase's own security standard. Medium is the correct calibration: too weak to justify high given limited remote exploitability, too material (public auth check, trivial safe fix mandated elsewhere) to downgrade to low.

surviving.title: Per-ad-set budget £1 floor breaks the campaign-budget sum invariant
surviving.evidence: `allocatedPence += share; budgets.set(adSet.id, Math.max(1, share / 100));` — allocatedPence accumulates the raw (un-floored) share, while the value stored per ad set is floored to a minimum of £1. The last ad set's share is computed as `penceToAllocate - allocatedPence` (line 552) from the un-floored running total.
surviving.failureScenario: Campaign budget £3 split across 3 implicit weighted ad sets where one weight yields a share < 100 pence (e.g. shares of 250p, 40p, 10p). The 40p and 10p ad sets are each stored as £1.00 (floored), while allocatedPence still records 40 and 10. The stored budgets sum to £2.50 + £1.00 + £1.00 = £4.50, i.e. £1.50 more than the £3 campaign budget — Meta will be sent per-ad-set budgets that exceed the intended campaign spend. Additionally the floor is £1 (100p), not the 1-pence minimum the pence-based maths implies.
surviving.rootCause: The £1 clamp is applied only to the stored value, not reconciled against allocatedPence or the campaign total, so the invariant 'sum of ad-set budgets == campaign budget' is silently violated whenever any weighted share falls below the floor.
surviving.fix: Clamp in pence and reconcile: enforce a per-ad-set minimum before splitting (e.g. reject or redistribute if campaignBudgetPence < 100 * implicitAdSets.length), and add the floor delta back into allocatedPence so the last ad set's remainder reflects the actual amounts stored — or compute the last share as campaignBudgetPence minus the sum of the already-stored (floored) shares.
surviving.siblingCheck: Cross-checked computeAdSetSpendCaps in src/lib/campaigns/food-budget-weighting.ts (the CBO path) which has an explicit sum-of-minimums preflight guarding the same invariant — the non-CBO allocateAdSetBudgets path has no equivalent reconciliation.
surviving.verifyReasoning:
The defect is real. In allocateAdSetBudgets (src/app/(app)/campaigns/[id]/actions.ts:548-556), allocatedPence accumulates the raw un-floored share (line 554: `allocatedPence += share`), while the value stored per ad set is floored to £1 minimum (line 555: `budgets.set(adSet.id, Math.max(1, share / 100))`). The last ad set's share is computed as `penceToAllocate - allocatedPence` (line 552) from that un-floored running total, so it never compensates for pence added by flooring earlier ad sets. Whenever any weighted share is below £1, the stored budgets sum to MORE than the campaign budget, and these per-ad-set budgets are sent directly to Meta as dailyBudget/lifetimeBudget in the non-CBO path (lines 926-927), so actual spend can exceed the owner's intended campaign total.

The invariant is genuine developer intent, not a reviewer invention: the code deliberately splits one campaign budget across ad sets, and the sibling CBO path computeAdSetSpendCaps (src/lib/campaigns/food-budget-weighting.ts:196-207) enforces exactly this with an explicit sum-of-minimums preflight that returns an error rather than silently clamping. The non-CBO allocateAdSetBudgets path has no equivalent reconciliation — confirming the sibling check in the claim.

Trigger realism: budget input allows min=£1 (CampaignBriefForm line 879), and only validates >0. With default weights (run-up=3, others=1, total 5), a weight-1 ad set's share drops below £1 when the campaign budget is under ~£5, and the LIFETIME/overlapping weighted path (not the DAILY sequential short-circuit at line 536) is the form default. So the scenario is a real, if uncommon, small-budget edge case.

Two minor caveats that do not defeat the finding: (1) the "£1 floor vs 1-pence" framing is slightly misleading — the £1 floor is intentional and matches META_MIN_AD_SET_BUDGET_GBP=1.0, so the unit is correct; the actual bug is the un-reconciled accumulation, which the claim also states correctly as the root cause. (2) The over-spend is bounded (under ~£1 per floored ad set, only at very small budgets), so it is a small-magnitude money leak rather than a large one. Medium severity is defensible for a silent over-spend on real ad money. The core defect holds on direct inspection.

surviving.title: brief.dayWeighting and manualDayWeights are collected and stored but never affect budget allocation (dead user input)
surviving.evidence: foodBookingBriefSchema validates `dayWeighting: z.enum(['even','boost_quiet','manual'])` and `manualDayWeights` (actions.ts:972-973) and persists the whole brief into source_snapshot. But the only consumer of these fields, computeFoodWindowWeights (food-budget-weighting.ts:95, which reads dayWeighting/manualDayWeights/bookingGapByService), is never called anywhere in src outside its own file. Ad-set budget_weight is written directly from the template weight at actions.ts:1262 and food-materialise.ts:673.
surviving.failureScenario: A user selects dayWeighting='boost_quiet' (or 'manual' with custom per-day multipliers) expecting Tuesday/Wednesday ad windows to receive a larger share of budget. The setting is validated, saved, and echoed in the snapshot, but budget_weight is the unmodified template weight, so spend allocation is identical to 'even'. The chosen weighting strategy silently has no effect on delivered budget, and manualDayWeights values are entirely ignored.
surviving.rootCause: Same missing wiring as the spend-cap finding: computeFoodWindowWeights (the function that consumes dayWeighting/manualDayWeights) is orphaned. The create path never runs it, so the user's weighting choice is inert.
surviving.fix: Wire computeFoodWindowWeights into createFoodBookingCampaign (and the materialise clone path) so dayWeighting and manualDayWeights actually shape the persisted budget_weight. Covered by the same fix as the spend-cap finding.
surviving.verifyReasoning: Verified end to end. (1) foodBookingBriefSchema validates dayWeighting (z.enum) and manualDayWeights at actions.ts:972-973 and the full brief is persisted into source_snapshot (actions.ts:1236). (2) A repo-wide grep for computeFoodWindowWeights returns only its own definition at food-budget-weighting.ts:95 — zero call sites anywhere in src/. This function is the sole consumer of dayWeighting/manualDayWeights (food-budget-weighting.ts:96-104), so with no caller those fields are inert. (3) The persisted budget_weight is written directly from window.budgetWeight at actions.ts:1262 (create path) and food-materialise.ts:673 (materialise path). (4) window.budgetWeight originates in calculateFoodBookingPhases (food-booking-phases.ts:86) as `budgetWeight: t.weight` — the static decision-stage template weight — and that function never reads dayWeighting or manualDayWeights from the brief. Grep of both food-booking-phases.ts and food-materialise.ts confirms no dayWeighting/manualDayWeights reference. (5) The only wired consumer of budgetWeight is computeAdSetSpendCaps (food-materialise.ts:614, flag-gated), which consumes budgetWeight but never dayWeighting/manualDayWeights, so it does not rescue the finding. The claim's facts, cited lines, and failure scenario all hold: a user selecting 'boost_quiet' or 'manual' per-day weights (surfaced in the UI at CampaignBriefForm.tsx) gets identical spend allocation to 'even'. This is genuine dead user input. Severity medium is appropriate: it is misleading user-facing behaviour that silently ignores a spend-allocation choice, not a crash or data-loss defect.

surviving.title: Management-app fallback bookings are invisible to ad-level pause logic and always add £0 value, causing wrong pause recommendations
surviving.evidence: In loadManagementBookingConversionEvents each fallback row is built with `utm_content: null` and `value: null` (lines 983-988). In buildBlendedBookingSignals: `const attributed = findAttributedAd(campaign, event.utm_content); if (!attributed) continue;` (lines 460-461) — a null utm_content always returns null from findAttributedAd, so the event never increments adBookings/adSetBookings. adHasTrackedBooking only consults `metric(ad.metrics_conversions) > 0 || (bookingSignal?.adBookings[ad.id] ?? 0) > 0` (line 1060).
surviving.failureScenario: An event campaign uses the management-app booking fallback (first-party booking_conversion_events empty, management connection enabled). Booking B for Ad X comes back from /api/marketing/event-booking-conversions with no utm_content. firstPartyBookings for the campaign becomes 1, so evaluateAdSetOptimisation enters the `adsWithBookings.length > 0` branch — but adBookings[X] is 0, so Ad X is treated as having NO tracked booking. If Ad X has spend >= £12 or >= 30 clicks it is recommended for pause even though it actually drove the management-tracked booking. firstPartyBookingValue also stays £0 for every management-sourced booking because event.value is hardcoded null.
surviving.rootCause: The management fallback endpoint does not return utm_content or a booking value, and the mapping hardcodes both to null; downstream ad-level attribution and value aggregation silently drop these bookings.
surviving.fix: Either request utm_content and value from the management endpoint and populate them in the mapped BookingConversionEventForOptimisation, or (if the endpoint cannot supply utm_content) exclude management-sourced bookings from the ad-set sibling-pause decision so an ad is not paused on the basis of a campaign-level booking that cannot be attributed to a specific ad. At minimum, gate the `adsWithBookings.length > 0` pause branch on ad-level attributable bookings only when the campaign's first-party signal came from an attributable source.
surviving.verifyReasoning:
The core defect is real and verified in src/lib/campaigns/optimisation.ts. Management-app fallback bookings are mapped with utm_content: null and value: null (lines 984, 988) — and structurally so, since ManagementBookingConversionRow (lines 110-118) does not even declare those fields. In buildBlendedBookingSignals, findAttributedAd returns null for null utm_content (line 1064), so each management booking hits `continue` (line 461) and never increments adBookings/adSetBookings. adHasTrackedBooking (line 1060) only consults Meta metrics_conversions or adBookings[ad.id], so an ad that genuinely drove a management-tracked booking is treated as having NO tracked booking. In evaluateAdSetOptimisation's sibling-pause branch (lines 397-412), that ad is then eligible for a pause recommendation at spend >= £12 or clicks >= 30 (constants confirmed at lines 31-32) despite having converted. Separately, firstPartyBookingValue/blendedBookingValue stay £0 for management bookings because event.value is hardcoded null (lines 470, 477). Both management and first-party events flow together into the signal builder (lines 856-857), so the path is reachable when the management fallback is enabled and first-party events are absent.

However, the claim's stated triggering mechanism is inaccurate: it says firstPartyBookings becoming 1 causes entry into the `adsWithBookings.length > 0` branch. That gate is NOT keyed on firstPartyBookings — it is keyed on per-ad adHasTrackedBooking. A management-only booking with null utm_content does not by itself trigger the branch; the wrong-pause outcome additionally requires a *sibling* ad in the same ad set to carry a tracked booking (a Meta metrics_conversions > 0, or an attributable first-party booking). This makes the failure conditional on a specific mix of attributed-and-unattributed bookings within one ad set, plus the spend/click threshold — narrower than the narrative implies. The "always £0 value" sub-claim is a genuine but lower-impact data-accuracy issue rather than a pause driver. The defect is genuine but the precise scenario is over-stated and more conditional, so I correct severity from high to medium.

surviving.title: Nightly dedup fails for low_ctr / high_cpc tracking issues because drifting metrics are embedded in the reason key
surviving.evidence: decisionKey keeps the full reason string for every action type except creative_fatigue: `return decision.actionType === 'creative_fatigue' ? identity : `${identity}:${decision.reason}`` (line 1029). But the low_ctr and high_cpc tracking-issue reasons embed live metric values: `CTR is ${metric(campaign.metrics_ctr).toFixed(2)}%...` (line 610) and `CPC is £${metric(campaign.metrics_cpc).toFixed(2)} after ${metric(campaign.metrics_clicks)} clicks...` (line 620). loadRecentOptimisationActionKeys builds the dedup set from the stored reason (lines 1015-1020).
surviving.failureScenario: A campaign has low CTR / high CPC that persists across nights but the numbers drift slightly (e.g. CPC £1.20/40 clicks one night, £1.35/47 clicks the next). Each night decisionKey produces a different key, existingKeys.has(...) returns false, and a fresh near-identical tracking_issue row is inserted into meta_optimisation_actions every single run — the exact WF-3 problem that was only patched for creative_fatigue. The optimisation actions feed (getRecentOptimisationActions) fills with duplicate low_ctr/high_cpc noise.
surviving.rootCause: WF-3 fixed reason-drift dedup only for creative_fatigue; low_ctr and high_cpc reasons also embed continuously changing metric values, so identity-plus-reason keying never matches across runs.
surviving.fix: Move the drifting numeric values out of the reason string used for keying (e.g. store the metric in recommendation_payload/metrics_snapshot and use a stable reason template), or extend the identity-only dedup branch in decisionKey to cover the metric-embedding tracking-issue categories (low_ctr, high_cpc) by keying on actionType + campaignId + category rather than the full reason.
surviving.verifyReasoning: Verified the full chain in src/lib/campaigns/optimisation.ts. (1) Cross-run dedup at lines 240-241 filters new decisions against existingKeys, which loadRecentOptimisationActionKeys (1015-1020) builds from the stored `reason` via decisionKey. (2) decisionKey (line 1029) uses identity-only keying ONLY for creative_fatigue; every other action type — including tracking_issue — appends the full reason string. (3) low_ctr (line 610) and high_cpc (line 620) are both actionType 'tracking_issue' (buildTrackingIssueDecision, line 718) and embed live metrics in the reason: low_ctr embeds metrics_ctr.toFixed(2); high_cpc embeds metrics_cpc.toFixed(2) AND raw metrics_clicks. Because metrics_clicks monotonically increases every night a campaign runs, the high_cpc reason string is essentially guaranteed to change each run even if CPC is stable, and low_ctr changes whenever CTR drifts by ≥0.01%. Each changed reason yields a new decisionKey, existingKeys.has(...) returns false, and a fresh near-duplicate tracking_issue row is inserted into meta_optimisation_actions every run — the exact WF-3 drift problem that was patched only for creative_fatigue. The getRecentOptimisationActions feed then fills with duplicate low_ctr/high_cpc entries. The failure scenario is reachable and unhandled. Severity 'medium' is fair: this is feed noise / UX degradation, not data corruption or a crash, so no severity inflation.

surviving.title: Same booking event is attributed to every active campaign that shares an event, inflating per-campaign first-party bookings
surviving.evidence: buildBlendedBookingSignals matches each campaign independently: `const matchedEvents = bookingEvents.filter((event) => bookingEventMatchesCampaign(event, campaign))` (line 452), and bookingEventMatchesCampaign matches on event_id/event_slug/short_code/utm_campaign (lines 1085-1100). There is no uniqueness constraint tying a booking event to a single campaign, and no constraint preventing two ACTIVE campaigns from carrying the same source_snapshot.eventId.
surviving.failureScenario: Two active campaigns (e.g. a warm-audience and a cold-audience push) both target eventId=E. Booking B for event E arrives once in booking_conversion_events. bookingEventMatchesCampaign returns true for BOTH campaigns, so firstPartyBookings=1 is credited to each. Campaign 2, which never actually produced booking B, is now treated as having a tracked booking — suppressing a legitimate copy_rewrite (evaluateCopyRewriteRecommendations returns early when blendedBookings > 0, line 648) or pause recommendation it should have received.
surviving.rootCause: Attribution is event-centric with no campaign-of-record on the booking event, so a booking that can only truly belong to one campaign is duplicated across every campaign sharing the event id/slug/short code.
surviving.fix: When a booking event carries a utm_content that resolves to a specific ad (and therefore a specific campaign), attribute it only to that campaign; for events with no utm_content, disambiguate by short_code or utm_campaign before falling back to event_id, and where still ambiguous credit at most one campaign (e.g. the most recently launched active campaign) rather than all of them.
surviving.verifyReasoning:
The defect is real and demonstrable from the code. In loadBlendedBookingSignals (lines 837-858), booking events are loaded once per account and passed as a single shared array to buildBlendedBookingSignals. That function (lines 444-487) iterates every campaign and independently filters the SAME shared array via bookingEventMatchesCampaign (line 452). bookingEventMatchesCampaign (lines 1077-1101) matches on event_id / event_slug / short_code / utm_campaign — all event-derived keys, none of which are unique to a single campaign. Campaigns are loaded with .eq('status','ACTIVE') (line 226), and nothing in the load or schema prevents two ACTIVE campaigns from sharing the same source_snapshot.eventId / source_id. Therefore, if an operator runs two active campaigns for the same event (a realistic warm/cold split), one real booking event is counted in matchedEvents for BOTH campaigns, giving each firstPartyBookings=1 (line 469). This propagates to blendedBookings via Math.max(metaBookings, firstPartyBookings) (line 476), and evaluateCopyRewriteRecommendations returns early at line 648 (`if (bookingSignal.blendedBookings > 0) return []`) after the critical-copy path — so a legitimate non-critical copy_rewrite recommendation for the phantom-attributed campaign is suppressed. The primary stated consequence is directly confirmed.

Two caveats that stop me short of fully endorsing the scenario as written, but do not refute the defect: (1) The pause-suppression element is overstated. The pause path (evaluateAdSetOptimisation, lines 389-421) keys off per-AD attribution adHasTrackedBooking → bookingSignal.adBookings[ad.id], and adBookings is only populated when findAttributedAd resolves the event's utm_content to a specific ad within that campaign (lines 460-463). The campaign-level phantom firstPartyBookings alone does not touch the pause path, so pause suppression only occurs in the narrower utm_content-resolves case. (2) Critical copy fixes (line 644) still fire because they precede the line-648 early return, so only softer rewrites are lost.

Net: the core mechanism (event-centric attribution with no campaign-of-record, duplicating a booking across every active campaign sharing the event) and its main consequence (suppressed copy_rewrite) are confirmed. Severity medium is honest — no data corruption or crash, degraded recommendation quality for the subset of accounts running multiple active campaigns per event.

surviving.title: No test asserts getCampaignWithTree blends first-party bookings or that dashboard and detail agree
surviving.evidence: applyFirstPartyBookingCount sets blendedBookings = Math.max(metaConversions, firstPartyBookings) and conversions/costPerConversion/conversionRate from it. getCampaignWithTree (actions.ts:1385-1390) calls loadDashboardFirstPartyBookingStats then applyFirstPartyBookingCount, mirroring getCampaignDashboard. No test in src references applyFirstPartyBookingCount, getCampaignWithTree, blendedBookings, or dbRowToCampaign.
surviving.failureScenario: The detail page (getCampaignWithTree) and the list/dashboard (getCampaignDashboard) must apply the SAME blend so Bookings / Cost-per-booking / Conv-rate agree. A future edit to applyFirstPartyBookingCount, to either call site's stats loading, or to the Math.max rule could silently make the two views disagree (e.g. detail shows Meta-only 0 while list shows 5) with no test catching it — grep finds zero coverage and there is no dashboard.test.ts.
surviving.rootCause: The recently-fixed booking-blend area has no unit test pinning the invariant that both entry points produce identical campaign-level blended metrics from the same first-party stats.
surviving.fix: Add src/lib/campaigns/dashboard.test.ts covering applyFirstPartyBookingCount: (1) blendedBookings = max(metaConversions, firstParty); (2) Meta 0 + firstParty 5 => conversions 5, costPerConversion spend/5, conversionRate (5/clicks)*100; (3) firstParty 0 leaves Meta metrics unchanged; (4) firstPartyBookingValue flows to blendedBookingValue. Then add a test (mocking the supabase client in app/actions) that getCampaignWithTree and buildCampaignDashboard, fed the same booking_conversion_events, produce identical campaign.performance.blendedBookings/conversions for a given campaign id.
surviving.siblingCheck: Grepped src for tests referencing applyFirstPartyBookingCount/getCampaignWithTree/blendedBookings/dbRowToCampaign — none. CampaignDashboard.test.tsx exercises buildCampaignDashboard for layout/food panels only (uses EMPTY_PERFORMANCE, never sets firstPartyBookingStats). food-cutoff-tuning.ts also has no test file.
surviving.verifyReasoning: Verified against the actual code. applyFirstPartyBookingCount (src/lib/campaigns/dashboard.ts:158-182) computes blendedBookings = Math.max(metaConversions, firstPartyBookings) and derives conversions/costPerConversion/conversionRate from it, exactly as claimed. getCampaignWithTree (src/app/(app)/campaigns/actions.ts:1385-1390) calls loadDashboardFirstPartyBookingStats then applyFirstPartyBookingCount, mirroring getCampaignDashboard (actions.ts:1425-1433 via buildCampaignDashboard). The invariant that detail and list views must apply the identical blend is real and explicitly documented in the code comment at actions.ts:1380-1384. Grep across all .test.ts/.test.tsx files finds zero references to applyFirstPartyBookingCount, getCampaignWithTree, blendedBookings, dbRowToCampaign, firstPartyBookingStats, firstPartyBookings, or metaConversions. No dashboard.test.ts exists in src/lib/campaigns/ (only generate.test.ts). CampaignDashboard.test.tsx uses EMPTY_PERFORMANCE and only exercises buildCampaignDashboard for layout/panels, never setting first-party stats — confirming the sibling-check. The finding is a legitimate missing-test gap on recently-changed, reporting-critical business logic with multiple branches (max rule, two zero-guards) in a trivially unit-testable pure function. Severity of medium is appropriate: it is a coverage gap, not a currently-misbehaving bug, so not higher; but the invariant is money/reporting-facing and recently touched, so not lower. No severity inflation. Every checkable claim holds.

surviving.title: Blended-vs-Meta conversion rate on campaign detail row can exceed 100% and mixes sources
surviving.evidence: conversionRate: campaign.performance.clicks > 0 ? (blendedBookings / campaign.performance.clicks) * 100 : 0,  // blendedBookings = Math.max(metaConversions, firstPartyBookings)
surviving.failureScenario: A campaign has 4 Meta link clicks but 6 first-party bookings attributed by short_code/event_id (bookings arrive without a counted Meta link click). getCampaignWithTree -> applyFirstPartyBookingCount sets blendedBookings=6, so conversionRate = (6/4)*100 = 150%. The /campaigns/[id] Performance matrix campaign row (page.tsx:611) renders '150.00%' as 'Conv. rate', a figure that is impossible for a click-through rate and mixes first-party bookings (numerator) with Meta link clicks (denominator).
surviving.rootCause: conversionRate is recomputed from blended bookings (which include first-party events attributed independently of Meta clicks) over Meta-only link clicks. Meta's own conversionRate uses Meta Purchase conversions over clicks and is bounded; the blend breaks that invariant.
surviving.fix: Either clamp the displayed rate (e.g. min(100, ...)) or, better, keep conversionRate Meta-only (conversions=metaConversions/clicks) and expose blended bookings separately, since the 'rate' is only meaningful for same-source numerator/denominator. Simplest safe fix: compute conversionRate from metaConversions rather than blendedBookings inside applyFirstPartyBookingCount.
surviving.verifyReasoning:
The defect holds on every load-bearing point.

1. Code path (dashboard.ts:164, 177-178): `blendedBookings = Math.max(metaConversions, firstPartyBookings)` and `conversionRate = (blendedBookings / campaign.performance.clicks) * 100`. When firstPartyBookings > clicks, this yields a rate above 100%.

2. Rendering (page.tsx:611): the campaign-variant row renders `formatPercentage(performance.conversionRate)` in the "Conv. rate" column. Verified the chain: page.tsx:39 calls `getCampaignWithTree`, which at actions.ts:1390 returns `applyFirstPartyBookingCount(campaign, stats.bookings, stats.value)` — so the campaign row's conversionRate is exactly the blended value.

3. Independence of numerator/denominator: `loadDashboardFirstPartyBookingStats` (actions.ts:1439) reads `booking_conversion_events` and passes them through `buildBlendedBookingSignals`, which attributes bookings by short_code / event_slug / utm_campaign / utm_content / fbclid / gclid — none of which require a counted Meta link click. So firstPartyBookings can legitimately exceed Meta `clicks`, making the >100% and cross-source-mixing scenario real (e.g. 6 bookings / 4 clicks = 150%).

The failure scenario is reproducible and the numerator (blended, includes first-party events attributed independently) genuinely mixes with a Meta-only denominator (clicks), breaking the click-through-rate invariant that Meta's own conversionRate (metaConversions/clicks) preserves.

Severity: this is display-only — a misleading/impossible percentage in one table cell. No crash, no data corruption, no wrong automated action (the totals row at line 309 recomputes from summed conversions/clicks and shares the same blend, but again only affects a displayed figure). "low" is the correct severity; there is no inflation to reject.

surviving.title: blendedBookingValue reports first-party value even when Meta bookings win the blend
surviving.evidence: const blendedBookings = Math.max(metaConversions, firstPartyBookings); ... blendedBookingValue: firstPartyBookingValue,
surviving.failureScenario: A campaign with metaConversions=10 and firstPartyBookings=2 gets blendedBookings=10 (Meta wins) but blendedBookingValue=firstPartyBookingValue (the value of just the 2 first-party bookings). blendedBookings and blendedBookingValue then describe different booking sets. Same pattern in buildBlendedBookingSignals (optimisation.ts:477). These fields are summed in addPerformance (dashboard.ts:456) and stored in optimisation metricsSnapshot, so any future consumer that divides value by bookings gets a wrong average-order-value.
surviving.rootCause: blendedBookingValue is hard-wired to firstPartyBookingValue regardless of which source supplied blendedBookings, and no Meta booking value is tracked, so the value field is only coherent when firstParty wins the max.
surviving.siblingCheck: Verified blendedBookingValue/firstPartyBookingValue are not currently rendered in CampaignDashboard.tsx or [id]/page.tsx (grep), so no user-facing impact today; risk is latent for the optimisation metricsSnapshot and any future value display.
surviving.fix: Only set blendedBookingValue when first-party bookings win the blend (blendedBookings === firstPartyBookings), otherwise 0/undefined; or document explicitly that blendedBookingValue is first-party-only and rename accordingly.
surviving.verifyReasoning:
Verified the code at dashboard.ts:158-182. Line 164 sets blendedBookings = Math.max(metaConversions, firstPartyBookings) while line 174 hard-wires blendedBookingValue: firstPartyBookingValue. When Meta wins the max (metaConversions > firstPartyBookings), blendedBookings counts the Meta set but blendedBookingValue reflects only first-party bookings — the two fields describe different booking sets, and no Meta booking value is tracked anywhere. The identical pattern is confirmed at optimisation.ts:476-477. Both fields propagate: they are summed in addPerformance (dashboard.ts:456) and stored in the optimisation metricsSnapshot (optimisation.ts:806).

Severity assessment: I searched all consumers. blendedBookingValue is NOT rendered in any UI (sibling check holds) and no code currently computes an average-order-value by dividing blendedBookingValue by blendedBookings — the only value/bookings division in the codebase is food-booking-insights.ts:170, which is spend/bookings (cost-per-booking) and unrelated. costPerConversion at dashboard.ts:176 correctly divides spend by blendedBookings, not by the value field, so it is unaffected. The incoherence is therefore real but latent: stored and aggregated but not yet consumed in a way that produces a visibly wrong number. This matches the claim's own characterisation (data-risk, low severity, no user-facing impact today, latent risk for the metricsSnapshot and any future AOV consumer). The claim is accurate and not severity-inflated.

surviving.title: Ingest accepts unbounded/future occurredAt; stale occurredAt becomes silently un-retryable and un-deliverable
surviving.failureScenario: A caller (or a delayed/replayed webhook) posts a booking with occurredAt older than 7 days. The event is stored, the initial CAPI send derives event_time from that timestamp, and Meta rejects it as outside the acceptance window (capi_status='failed'). The retry cron only selects rows with `occurred_at >= now-6.5d` (retry-capi-conversions/route.ts:54), so the failed row is never retried and never heals; a future-dated occurredAt would likewise produce an invalid event_time. The failure is invisible — the row sits at capi_status='failed' with no alerting.
surviving.rootCause: No upper/lower sanity bound on occurredAt at ingest, and Meta's acceptance window is only enforced implicitly by the retry query rather than validated at write time; a value outside [now-7d, now] can never be delivered nor retried.
surviving.siblingCheck: Retry window RETRY_WINDOW_MS/windowStart at src/app/api/cron/retry-capi-conversions/route.ts:11,45,54; event_time derivation at src/lib/meta/conversions-api.ts:87.
surviving.fix: Validate/clamp occurredAt at ingest: reject or clamp values older than ~6.5 days and reject future timestamps, so every stored event is deliverable and retryable within Meta's window.
surviving.verifyReasoning:
The code facts are accurate and the failure chain is genuinely reachable. Ingest (route.ts:44) validates occurredAt only as ISO-8601 format with no lower/upper bound, and defaults to now (line 119). event_time is derived straight from occurredAt (conversions-api.ts:87). Meta CAPI rejects events with event_time older than ~7 days — a constraint the codebase itself asserts in the retry cron comment (retry-capi-conversions/route.ts:9-11). When a stale occurredAt is supplied, the initial send fails at Meta (conversions-api.ts:115-120) and the row is written as capi_status='failed' (route.ts:218). The retry cron then filters .gte('occurred_at', now-6.5d) (route.ts:54), which permanently excludes any row whose occurred_at is older than 6.5 days. So a stale-dated failed row is never retried and never heals — un-deliverable and un-retryable, sitting silently at 'failed'. This is exactly the described defect.

Two caveats that keep severity at low (not higher): (1) the normal client path defaults occurredAt to now, so the stale value only arises from a delayed/replayed webhook or a buggy/malicious caller, and ingest is gated behind BOOKING_CONVERSION_INGEST_SECRET (a trusted first-party integration), limiting likelihood; (2) impact is a silently dropped conversion event (attribution data loss) — no crash, no security exposure. The claim's 'future-dated becomes un-retryable' sub-point is slightly imprecise (a future occurred_at stays >= windowStart, so it keeps being retried and keeps failing rather than being excluded — it burns retries but is not un-retryable), but the load-bearing stale-date claim is fully correct. Real defect, severity 'low' is appropriate.

surviving.title: Initial CAPI-status update in ingest is unguarded, so a concurrent retry can regress a sent event
surviving.failureScenario: The site re-posts the same bookingId (idempotent upsert on account_id,booking_id) while the retry cron is processing that row. Both writers update by (account_id, booking_id) with no status precondition. If the cron marks it 'sent' and the slower ingest response then writes 'failed' (its own send raced/errored), a genuinely-sent event is downgraded to 'failed' and re-sent by the next cron run — capi_sent_at/status become misleading and cost-per-booking flips (Meta dedups on event_id but the recorded state is wrong).
surviving.rootCause: Both writers update the row unconditionally by primary key with no optimistic guard, so last-writer-wins can regress a successful send.
surviving.siblingCheck: Retry writer at src/app/api/cron/retry-capi-conversions/route.ts:105-118 also updates by (account_id,booking_id) with no status guard; the no-consent skip at route.ts:178-183 correctly guards with `.is('capi_status', null)`, showing the guard pattern exists but isn't applied to the send-status writes.
surviving.fix: Guard both status writes so a 'sent' row is never regressed, e.g. add `.neq('capi_status', 'sent')` to the update at route.ts:229 and to the cron update, or only overwrite when the new status is 'sent'.
surviving.verifyReasoning:
The code facts all hold. In src/app/api/booking-conversions/route.ts the send-status update (lines 215-230) filters only on `.eq('account_id', accountId).eq('booking_id', parsedPayload.bookingId)` with no `capi_status` precondition. The sibling retry writer at src/app/api/cron/retry-capi-conversions/route.ts (lines 105-118) is identical — updates by (account_id, booking_id) with no status guard. The no-consent skip (route.ts:178-183) DOES guard with `.is('capi_status', null)`, so the guard pattern demonstrably exists but was not applied to the send-status writes — confirming this is an inconsistency, not intentional design.

The failure scenario is a valid last-writer-wins race: the ingest upsert is keyed `onConflict: 'account_id,booking_id'` (line 167), so an idempotent re-post targets the same row the cron is processing. The retry cron's selection filter (line 53) includes `capi_status.eq.failed`, so a row wrongly downgraded from 'sent' to 'failed' is re-picked and re-sent on the next run. The result — `capi_status='failed'`/`capi_sent_at=null` on a genuinely-sent event plus a redundant send — is a real data-integrity regression.

Severity is correctly self-rated low and confidence medium: the race window is narrow (requires a concurrent re-post whose own CAPI send errors, interleaved to land after the cron's 'sent' write), and metaEventId is deterministic per booking (line 118) so Meta dedups on event_id, protecting the business metric (cost-per-booking) from double-counting. The harm is confined to misleading local state and a redundant network call. No severity inflation; the fix (add `.neq('capi_status', 'sent')` to both writes) is sound and mirrors the existing guard. Marked CONFIRMED rather than PLAUSIBLE because every code-level premise is verified and the regression is a direct logical consequence of two unguarded writes on the same key; only the production timing cannot be independently reproduced.

surviving.title: Rollback pauses Meta ad creatives, which Meta rejects — creatives are never cleaned up on failure
surviving.evidence: createdMetaObjects includes creative ids: `createdMetaObjects.push(creative.id)` (line 1033). Rollback loop calls pauseMetaObject on every id: `for (const metaObjectId of Array.from(new Set([...activatedMetaObjects, ...createdMetaObjects]))) { await pauseMetaObject(metaObjectId, accessToken); }`. pauseMetaObject -> setMetaObjectStatus POSTs `{ status: 'PAUSED' }` to `/{objectId}` (src/lib/meta/marketing.ts line 489-506), but ad creatives are not deliverable objects and have no ACTIVE/PAUSED status field.
surviving.failureScenario: Publish fails partway. Rollback tries to POST status=PAUSED to each created adcreative id; Meta returns an 'Invalid parameter' error, which is caught and logged per-object ('[publishCampaign] Rollback failed for <id>') and skipped. The creative objects remain in the ad account permanently. Because meta_creative_id was persisted but the campaign is reset to DRAFT, the next publish attempt (see other finding) creates fresh creatives, compounding the orphans.
surviving.rootCause: The rollback strategy is pause-only and treats every created object identically, but ad creatives are non-pausable and can only be removed via DELETE. There is no delete path and no creative-specific handling.
surviving.fix: Track creative ids separately from pausable objects (adsets/ads/campaign). During rollback, pause the deliverable objects but DELETE creatives (Meta DELETE /{creative_id}); or, if deletion is undesired, exclude creatives from the rollback loop and rely on the resume path reusing meta_creative_id (paired with the fix above) so they are not orphaned.
surviving.siblingCheck: Reviewed all createdMetaObjects.push calls (campaign 875, adset 943, creative 1033, ad 1050) and setMetaObjectStatus in marketing.ts — only creatives lack a status lifecycle, so only they fail to roll back.
surviving.verifyReasoning: Verified against the cited code. Creative ids ARE pushed into createdMetaObjects (actions.ts:1033) and the rollback loop (actions.ts:1133) calls pauseMetaObject on every id, which via setMetaObjectStatus (marketing.ts:496-506) POSTs {status:'PAUSED'} to /{objectId}. The Meta Marketing API AdCreative node's status enum is {ACTIVE, IN_PROCESS, WITH_ISSUES, DELETED} — there is no PAUSED value, and adcreatives are not deliverable objects with an ACTIVE/PAUSED lifecycle. So POST {status:'PAUSED'} to an adcreative id returns an invalid-parameter error, which is caught and logged per-object at actions.ts:1136-1139 and skipped. The creative therefore is not rolled back and remains in the ad account. I confirmed there is NO delete path anywhere (grep found only metaPost helpers; no DELETE/deleteMetaObject), and createMetaAdCreative (actions.ts:1017) is called unconditionally inside the ads loop with no read/reuse of the persisted meta_creative_id — so a republish after a DRAFT reset creates fresh creatives, compounding orphans exactly as claimed. The sibling check is also sound: only creatives among the pushed object types lack a PAUSED status. The defect is real (CONFIRMED). However I downgrade severity from medium to low: the actual pausable deliverables (ad, adset, campaign) DO get paused so delivery and spend are stopped — orphaned creatives do not spend money or deliver on their own, so the impact is ad-account clutter plus accumulating orphans on retries, not a data-loss or billing risk. That is real but cosmetic/hygiene-level rather than medium data-risk; the 'data-risk' framing and medium rating are mildly inflated, but the underlying bug holds.

surviving.title: createFoodBookingCampaign never validates input.startDate, so a malformed date persists a broken DRAFT with null-dated ad sets and a null campaign end_date
surviving.evidence: `const allWindows = calculateFoodBookingPhases(brief, input.startDate);` is called with no format check on input.startDate (only brief is Zod-parsed at line 1082; geoRadius/audienceMode/budget are validated, startDate is not). In food-booking-phases.ts:56 `DateTime.fromISO(campaignStartDate,{zone}).startOf('day')` yields an invalid DateTime for a malformed value; the `runDate < start` guard (line 70) is false against an invalid DateTime, so windows are pushed with `runDate: runDate.toISODate()!` = null and `serviceDate: null`.
surviving.failureScenario: createFoodBookingCampaign is invoked (it is an exported server action, callable with arbitrary payloads) with input.startDate='' or a non-ISO string. calculateFoodBookingPhases returns windows whose runDate/serviceDate are null; enabledWindows is non-empty; endDate=lastWindow.runDate=null. The campaign row inserts with end_date=null and each ad_set inserts phase_start=null/phase_end=null, leaving an orphaned, un-publishable DRAFT campaign (dates broken) rather than a clean validation error. Sibling actions validate this: saveCampaignDraft -> calculateInclusiveDurationDays -> parseDateOnly throws on non-YYYY-MM-DD.
surviving.rootCause: The food path skips the ISO-date guard that phases.ts/parseDateOnly enforces for event/evergreen campaigns; Luxon's invalid-DateTime comparisons silently pass the runDate<start filter and toISODate()! returns null under a non-null assertion.
surviving.fix: At the top of createFoodBookingCampaign (before calculateFoodBookingPhases), validate input.startDate with the same guard used elsewhere, e.g. `if (!/^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$/.test(input.startDate) || !DateTime.fromISO(input.startDate,{zone:'Europe/London'}).isValid) throw new Error('Start date must be a valid YYYY-MM-DD date.')`. Alternatively have calculateFoodBookingPhases throw on an invalid start DateTime instead of returning null-dated windows.
surviving.verifyReasoning:
The defect is real and I verified every step. createFoodBookingCampaign (actions.ts:1071) is an exported server action that validates brief, geoRadiusMiles, audienceMode and budgetAmount but never validates input.startDate before passing it to calculateFoodBookingPhases (line 1128). I confirmed the Luxon mechanics by executing against the project's luxon: DateTime.fromISO('', {zone}) is invalid, so the `runDate < start` guard (food-booking-phases.ts:70) evaluates false (does not drop windows) and toISODate() returns null, masked by the `!` non-null assertions at lines 81/84. A full-loop simulation reproduced non-empty enabledWindows with runDate:null/serviceDate:null and endDate:null. Those nulls flow into the inserts: meta_campaigns.end_date (line 1225) and ad_sets.phase_start/phase_end (lines 1256-1257). The sibling event/evergreen path does enforce format via parseDateOnly (phases.ts:126-129, regex ^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$), which the food path skips — exactly as claimed.

Two caveats reduce severity but not existence: (1) the UI form (CampaignBriefForm.tsx:116) validates startDate presence, so a normal browser user is unlikely to trigger this; it needs a malformed/crafted payload to the exported action or a future non-form caller. (2) I could not confirm DB nullability of end_date/phase_start because the base tables come from an out-of-chain v1 baseline not present in supabase/migrations. If those columns are NOT NULL, the outcome is a DB error with deletePartialCampaignDraft rollback (line 1273) rather than a persisted orphaned DRAFT — so the specific "broken DRAFT persists" claim is not fully provable, but the underlying missing-validation defect holds regardless. Given the narrow exploit surface (not reachable via normal UI) and the uncertain worst-case, medium is inflated; low is the accurate severity.

surviving.title: RecurringControls status contract mismatch — expects lowercase statuses that meta_campaigns never produces, and its actions target the wrong table
surviving.evidence: `const isRecurring = ['weekly', 'weekly_recurring', 'daily', 'monthly'].includes(campaignType);` and status branches check `status === 'scheduled'` (line 127), `status === 'paused'` (line 144), `status === 'completed'` (line 121). The page passes `status={campaign.status}` where campaign.status is `CampaignStatus = 'DRAFT'|'ACTIVE'|'PAUSED'|'ARCHIVED'` (uppercase, actions.ts:2172) and `campaignType={campaign.campaignType ?? campaign.campaignKind}` which for meta_campaigns is 'event'/'evergreen'/'food_booking'. Additionally pauseRecurringCampaign/resumeRecurringCampaign/stopRecurringCampaign (src/app/actions/campaigns.ts:26,73,120) query `.from('campaigns')` by `.eq('id', campaignId)`, but the page's campaignId is a `meta_campaigns.id`.
surviving.failureScenario: Even if the page.tsx:105 guard were corrected to render RecurringControls for a recurring meta_campaign: `isRecurring` would be false (campaignType is 'event'/etc.), so it returns null. If that too were fixed, the status branches compare uppercase 'PAUSED' against lowercase 'paused' and never match, so no button appears. And if a button did fire, `stopRecurringCampaign(metaCampaignId)` does `.from('campaigns').eq('id', metaCampaignId).single()` -> 'Campaign not found', so the action fails against the wrong table.
surviving.rootCause: The component was built for the `campaigns` table (lowercase status enum: scheduled/paused/completed, campaign_type: weekly/daily/monthly) but is wired into the meta_campaigns-backed detail page. The status enums and the target table both diverge.
surviving.siblingCheck: Confirmed campaigns-table status values in src/app/actions/campaigns.ts (scheduled/paused/completed) vs meta_campaigns CampaignStatus (DRAFT/ACTIVE/PAUSED/ARCHIVED) in types/campaigns.ts and dbRowToCampaign. Verified the recurring actions all target `.from('campaigns')`.
surviving.fix: Align the component to one data model. If it must serve the meta_campaigns detail page, translate/accept the uppercase CampaignStatus and route to actions that operate on meta_campaigns. Otherwise remove it from this page. Add a shared status type so the client gate and server action agree.
surviving.verifyReasoning:
All cited facts are verified against the code. (1) getCampaignWithTree (actions.ts:1369) reads from meta_campaigns, so the page's campaign.id is a meta_campaigns.id. (2) dbRowToCampaign sets status: row.status as CampaignStatus (uppercase DRAFT/ACTIVE/PAUSED/ARCHIVED, line 2173) and campaignType = row.campaign_type ?? row.campaign_kind ?? null (line 2190), where campaign_kind for meta_campaigns is event/evergreen/food_booking. (3) RecurringControls.isRecurring (line 35) checks ['weekly','weekly_recurring','daily','monthly'].includes(campaignType) — false for meta_campaigns values, so it returns null. (4) Its status branches compare against lowercase 'scheduled'/'paused'/'completed' (lines 121,127,144), which never match uppercase CampaignStatus. (5) pauseRecurringCampaign/resumeRecurringCampaign/stopRecurringCampaign (campaigns.ts:24,72,116) all query .from('campaigns') with lowercase status semantics, a different table from meta_campaigns. The component is genuinely built for the legacy `campaigns` table and mis-wired into the meta_campaigns-backed detail page — a real contract/table mismatch.

However, severity is inflated. The page-level render guard (page.tsx:105) gates RecurringControls on ['weekly','weekly_recurring','daily','monthly'].includes(campaign.campaignKind), and campaignKind is typed PaidCampaignKind = 'event'|'evergreen'|'food_booking' — so the guard is ALWAYS false and the component NEVER renders on this page. The claim itself concedes every failure branch is prefaced by 'even if the guard were corrected'. So there is zero current user-facing impact: no button ever appears, no wrong-table action ever fires. This is a latent/dead-wiring defect, not an active functional bug, which caps it below the medium (active malfunction) framing. Real defect, over-rated severity — corrected to low.

surviving.title: No error boundary for campaigns routes — a DB/Meta load failure throws an unhandled exception instead of a user-facing error state
surviving.evidence: CampaignDetailPage awaits `getCampaignWithTree(id)` which does `if (error) throw error;` (actions.ts:1375). CampaignsPage (page.tsx:11) awaits `getCampaignDashboard()` which does `if (campaignsError) throw campaignsError;` (actions.ts:1422). There is no error.tsx or loading.tsx in `src/app/(app)/campaigns/`, `src/app/(app)/campaigns/[id]/`, or `src/app/(app)/`, and no error.tsx/global-error.tsx anywhere in src/app (find returned nothing).
surviving.failureScenario: Supabase has a transient outage or the `meta_campaigns` query errors. The thrown error is not caught by any route error boundary, so the user sees Next.js's default unstyled error screen (or a blank fallback) with no retry affordance, instead of a branded 'Could not load campaigns' state. The ui-patterns rule explicitly requires every data-driven UI to handle the error state.
surviving.rootCause: Server-component data loaders throw on failure but the route group has no `error.tsx` boundary and no per-route loading UI, so failures and slow loads have no designed presentation.
surviving.siblingCheck: Searched entire src/app for error.tsx/global-error.tsx/loading.tsx in these route groups; none exist. This is scoped here because both campaigns loaders re-throw.
surviving.fix: Add `src/app/(app)/campaigns/error.tsx` (client component with a reset button and branded messaging) and optionally `loading.tsx` skeletons. This satisfies the loading/error-state requirement without changing behaviour.
surviving.verifyReasoning: Verified against source. Both loaders re-throw on DB error: getCampaignWithTree at actions.ts:1375 (`if (error) throw error;`), awaited by CampaignDetailPage; getCampaignDashboard at actions.ts:1422 (`if (campaignsError) throw campaignsError;`), awaited by CampaignsPage. A `find` for error.tsx/global-error.tsx/loading.tsx across src/app returned nothing — no route error boundary or loading UI exists anywhere in the tree. The (app) layout only wraps its own sidebar health/notification queries in try/catch; children errors propagate past it. With no error.tsx boundary at any level, a thrown Supabase/Meta error falls through to Next.js's built-in error handling rather than a branded 'Could not load campaigns' state with a retry affordance. This is a genuine violation of the project's ui-patterns rule requiring every data-driven UI to handle the error state. Severity 'low' is appropriate and not inflated: it only manifests on a transient outage/query failure (not the happy path), causes no data loss or security issue, and Next still renders a fallback — it is a missing designed error/loading presentation, exactly as described. The 'or a blank fallback' phrasing is slightly loose but does not undermine the core, accurate finding. Fix (add error.tsx and optionally loading.tsx) is safe and behaviour-preserving.

surviving.title: Optimise/sync cron routes return HTTP 200 even when every account or campaign fails
