surviving.evidence: The optimise route catches per-account errors into `results.push({ accountId, error })` (lines 58-64) then always returns `NextResponse.json({ accounts: results.length, results })` with an implicit 200 (line 67). sync-meta-campaigns/route.ts does the same: per-campaign failures are pushed to failedCampaignIds and the handler returns `NextResponse.json({ synced, failed, failedCampaignIds })` at 200 (route.ts:41).
surviving.failureScenario: Every account's optimisation throws (e.g. all Meta tokens expired, or a stale action_type CHECK constraint makes every insert fail). The QStash/cron scheduler receives HTTP 200 and records the job as successful, so no alert fires and the optimiser silently stops producing actions until someone notices manually. failedActionInserts and per-account errors live only in the JSON body, which cron monitors typically do not inspect.
surviving.rootCause: The routes treat any completion of the loop as success and never translate an all-failed (or partially-failed) batch into a non-2xx status or a logged alert.
surviving.fix: Return a 5xx (or 207-style flag the monitor checks) when results contains errors for all accounts, or when failedActionInserts/failed counts are non-zero, so the scheduler's success/failure signal reflects the actual outcome; alternatively emit a structured error log that the alerting pipeline watches.
surviving.verifyReasoning: The defect holds as described. Both routes catch per-item failures and return an implicit HTTP 200 regardless of outcome: optimise-meta-campaigns/route.ts pushes per-account errors into `results` (lines 58-64) and returns `NextResponse.json({ accounts, results })` at line 67 with no status override; sync-meta-campaigns/route.ts pushes failures into `failedCampaignIds` (line 36) and returns `{ synced, failed, failedCampaignIds }` at line 41, also 200. Both routes DO return 500 for the top-level account/campaign query error (optimise line 24), proving the authors know how to signal failure via status — but the all-failed / partial-failed loop case never translates to a non-2xx. These are registered Vercel Cron jobs (vercel.json), not QStash as the claim states; that detail is wrong, but it does not invalidate the defect because Vercel Cron keys job health off the HTTP status code, so a 200 with an all-error body is recorded as a successful run. The concrete scenario is realistic: if every Meta token is expired, every `runMetaCampaignOptimisation`/`syncMetaCampaignPerformance` call throws, all are caught and logged via console.error, and the route still returns 200 — no alert fires. The repo's own defect log independently corroborates this (tasks/fix-function/2026-07-07-campaigns/defect-log.md:64: "cron reports aggregate only; partial failures invisible"). Failures are visible only in Vercel logs via console.error, not as a monitorable failure signal. Severity "low" is correct and not inflated: this is an observability/alerting gap, not data loss, security, or a functional break — it degrades gracefully with per-item logging. Minor caveat noted (QStash mislabel; Vercel Cron alerting behaviour on 2xx is a well-established assumption rather than something provable from this repo alone), but the code-level defect — no non-2xx status or alert on an all-failed or partially-failed batch — is fully confirmed by reading the two files.

surviving.title: dbRowToCampaign reads meta_campaigns.campaign_type and .auto_confirm columns that do not exist
surviving.evidence:
campaignType: row.campaign_type ?? row.campaign_kind ?? null,
autoConfirm: Boolean(row.auto_confirm ?? false),  // CampaignDbRow declares campaign_type/auto_confirm (lines 1998-1999) but meta_campaigns has neither column
surviving.failureScenario: getCampaigns/getCampaignWithTree do select('*') from meta_campaigns. The row never contains campaign_type or auto_confirm (they exist only on the organic `campaigns` table). campaignType therefore always collapses to campaign_kind (event/evergreen/food_booking) and autoConfirm is always false. Any consumer trusting these to reflect recurring cadence or auto-publish on a paid campaign gets silently wrong values — the detail page's autoConfirm badge would always read 'Manual approval', and campaignType can never equal 'weekly'.
surviving.rootCause: CampaignDbRow and dbRowToCampaign were written as if meta_campaigns carried the organic campaigns table's columns. The `?? row.campaign_kind` fallback and Boolean(... ?? false) mask the missing columns, so no runtime error surfaces — the fields just read meaningless-for-recurring data.
surviving.fix: Remove campaign_type/auto_confirm from CampaignDbRow and dbRowToCampaign (default campaignType to campaign_kind and autoConfirm to false explicitly, with a comment noting meta_campaigns has no such columns), or add the columns via migration and select them if recurring paid campaigns are intended. select('*') will not error either way, which is why the bug is invisible.
surviving.siblingCheck: Confirmed against v1_baseline.sql/remote-v1-backup.sql: meta_campaigns has campaign_kind (constraint event|evergreen) but no campaign_type/auto_confirm; those exist only on `campaigns` and `content_items`. The campaign_effectiveness_upgrade migration adds quality_* columns only, not these.
surviving.verifyReasoning:
The technical facts are CONFIRMED but the stated failure scenario is REFUTED, so the finding is real-but-overstated (PLAUSIBLE at reduced severity).

Confirmed facts:
- meta_campaigns has NO campaign_type or auto_confirm column. Verified in supabase/baseline/v1_baseline.sql (campaign_kind at line 326 with CHECK constraint event|evergreen at line 345; campaign_type/auto_confirm exist only on the organic `campaigns` table at lines 195/199). No migration adds these columns to meta_campaigns — 20260606170000_campaign_effectiveness_upgrade.sql adds only quality_* and audience_strategy (lines 10-14), and no migration widens the campaign_kind constraint.
- getCampaigns (actions.ts:1346-1347) and getCampaignWithTree (1369-1370) use select('*'), which returns only real meta_campaigns columns, so row.campaign_type and row.auto_confirm are always undefined.
- dbRowToCampaign (lines 2190-2191) therefore always collapses campaignType to campaign_kind and autoConfirm to false. CampaignDbRow (1998-1999) declares columns that never populate — a genuine type-level defect / dead mapping.

Why the failure scenario is refuted (severity inflation):
- The two claimed user-facing symptoms — "autoConfirm badge always reads Manual approval" and "campaignType can never equal weekly" — both live inside RecurringControls (recurring-controls.tsx lines 35, 101-116). That component is only mounted when campaign.campaignKind is in ['weekly','weekly_recurring','daily','monthly'] (page.tsx:105). But meta_campaigns.campaign_kind is constrained to event|evergreen, so that guard is never true and RecurringControls NEVER renders for a paid campaign. There is no visible wrong badge because the component never appears on screen.
- The other campaignType/autoConfirm consumers found by grep (link-in-bio, planner/data.ts:831, content.ts:40) read from the organic `campaigns`/`content_items` tables, not from this dbRowToCampaign path, so they are unaffected by this mapping.

Net: a real but inert defect — a misleading type declaration and dead field mapping masked by select('*'), producing no observable incorrect behaviour. The described failure cannot actually occur. Genuine enough not to reject outright, but severity should be low (type/hygiene cleanup), not medium.

rejected.title: blendedBookingValue discards Meta value and is 0 when first-party bookings carry no value
rejected.verifyReasoning:
The cited line (`src/lib/campaigns/dashboard.ts:174`, `blendedBookingValue: firstPartyBookingValue`) exists exactly as quoted, but the claimed defect does not hold.

Core premise refuted: the failure scenario asserts "Meta may have reported a purchase value" that `blendedBookingValue` discards, causing a count/value disagreement and wrong ROAS. There is NO Meta monetary value anywhere in the system to discard:
- The Meta insights query (`src/lib/meta/marketing.ts:534`) requests `fields: 'spend,impressions,reach,clicks,inline_link_clicks,ctr,cpc,actions,cost_per_action_type'` — it never requests `action_values` (Meta's monetary purchase-value field). `sumPurchaseActions` (line 591) extracts a purchase COUNT, not a value; `cost_per_action_type` is cost, not conversion value.
- `CampaignPerformanceMetrics` (`src/types/campaigns.ts:110-125`) has only `firstPartyBookingValue`/`blendedBookingValue`; there is no `metaBookingValue`/`metaConversionValue` field. A grep for metaBookingValue/purchaseValue/action_values across src returned nothing. The optimisation sibling (`optimisation.ts`) likewise has `metaBookings` (count) but no meta value.
- Therefore the proposed fix `metaBookingValue ?? 0` references a value that does not exist; the count uses `max(meta, firstParty)` only because Meta reports a count, and there is genuinely no Meta value counterpart to blend.

User-visibility refuted: `blendedBookingValue` is never rendered in the campaigns UI. `CampaignDashboard.tsx` displays only booking COUNTS (`formatBookingMetric` uses `blendedBookings`); there is no ROAS, value column, or currency-of-value display. `blendedBookingValue` is only summed into totals (dashboard.ts:456) and passed through optimisation signals — neither surfaces a "5 bookings worth £0.00" contradiction to any user.

Residual behaviour (null-valued first-party bookings yield value 0 while count is 5) is internally consistent — both come from the same first-party source, and value legitimately being 0/unknown is not a data-loss bug. The defect as characterised (discarding a Meta value, user-visible count/value disagreement, wrong ROAS) cannot occur. Severity "medium/high confidence" is inflated for an invisible, internally-consistent field with no competing data source.

rejected.title: 'No ad sets published' early return skips rollback and leaves the created Meta campaign orphaned
rejected.verifyReasoning:
The claimed failure scenario cannot actually occur, so the "orphaned Meta campaign" data-risk is not realizable.

Preflight (line 780, backed by validatePublishPreflight at 1160-1161) returns an error whenever adSets.length === 0, so by the time execution reaches the guard at line 1069, adSets.length > 0 is guaranteed and the loop at 893-958 ran at least once.

Inside that loop, every ad set follows exactly one of two paths: (a) adSet.meta_adset_id is already set → successfulAdSets++ at line 914; or (b) createMetaAdSet is called — on success successfulAdSets++ at line 944, on failure it throws, which jumps straight to the catch block at 1112 where the unified rollback (pause created objects) and DRAFT reset run. There is no continue, guard, or branch in the ad-set loop that skips the increment. The inner ads loop's continue (line 973) does not affect successfulAdSets.

Therefore any path that reaches line 1069 has necessarily incremented successfulAdSets once per ad set, giving successfulAdSets >= adSets.length >= 1. The condition (adSets.length > 0 && successfulAdSets === 0) is unsatisfiable at that point. No fresh publish can create the Meta campaign, skip every ad set with successfulAdSets staying 0, and reach the early return — so no orphaned PAUSED Meta campaign shell can be produced by this path.

The claim itself concedes the guard is "largely unreachable today because each ad set either increments successfulAdSets or throws." That concession is the whole ballgame: this is dead defensive code, and the early-return-vs-throw distinction is a code-style nicety, not a live data-loss risk. Rating it a medium data-risk is severity inflation on an unreachable path. The suggested throw would be a marginal robustness improvement but fixes no reachable defect.

rejected.title: blendedBookingValue reports £0 whenever bookings come only from Meta, contradicting a non-zero blended booking count
rejected.verifyReasoning:
The code at optimisation.ts:476-477 and 805-806 is exactly as quoted: blendedBookings uses Math.max(metaBookings, firstPartyBookings) while blendedBookingValue is purely firstPartyBookingValue, so when Meta reports N conversions and no first-party events have landed, the snapshot does hold blendedBookings=N with blendedBookingValue=0. That factual description is accurate.

The claim is rejected on the failure scenario, which cannot actually occur, and on inflated severity:

1. No consumer reads blendedBookingValue back from the snapshot. metrics_snapshot is written at optimisation.ts:261/721 and read into OptimisationActionSummary as an opaque `Record` (actions.ts: metricsSnapshot: row.metrics_snapshot ?? {}). Grepping every snapshot.* / Snapshot.* access shows only bookingConversionOptimised, bookingUrl, bookingMode are ever read back — never blendedBookingValue. It is a write-only diagnostic blob.

2. blendedBookingValue is never rendered in any UI. Searching src/features for BookingValue/bookingValue returns nothing. The only dashboard render of blended figures (CampaignDashboard.tsx:1422-1430) shows booking counts only ("5 (0 first-party)"), never a monetary value. So "any UI or downstream consumer reading the snapshot sees '5 bookings worth £0'" is false — no UI or logic surfaces the pair.

3. The behaviour is by-design and semantically defensible: Meta does not return a first-party monetary value, so there is genuinely no Meta-side value to blend. blendedBookingValue faithfully means "value of bookings we could actually attribute a £ figure to." The dashboard.ts path (lines 164/174) does the identical thing, confirming it is an intentional convention, not an overlooked reconciliation.

The result is at most a latent labelling nicety with zero realised harm — no user sees it, no decision consumes it. The stated failure scenario does not manifest, and "medium" severity is inflated for a value that is never displayed or used downstream. Per the strict-default instruction, this is REJECTED.

rejected.title: Recurring pause/resume/stop actions query the wrong table (campaigns) and set a status the constraint rejects (paused)
rejected.verifyReasoning:
The claim's core, load-bearing failure scenario cannot occur. It asserts that RecurringControls passes a meta_campaigns row id into pauseRecurringCampaign, which queries `.from('campaigns')` and returns 'Campaign not found' for every paid campaign. But the detail page (src/app/(app)/campaigns/[id]/page.tsx:105) only renders RecurringControls when `['weekly','weekly_recurring','daily','monthly'].includes(campaign.campaignKind)`. On a meta_campaigns row, `campaignKind` is typed `PaidCampaignKind = 'event' | 'evergreen' | 'food_booking'` (src/types/campaigns.ts:14, 284) — none of the four gate values can ever match. So the component never renders on the paid detail page and the meta_campaigns id is never passed to these actions. A second independent barrier: RecurringControls gates its Pause/Resume/Stop buttons on lowercase statuses ('scheduled'/'paused'), while campaign.status is UPPERCASE CampaignStatus ('DRAFT'|'ACTIVE'|'PAUSED'|'ARCHIVED', src/types/campaigns.ts:8, 280) — so even if it rendered, no button would appear. I confirmed via grep that recurring-controls.tsx is the ONLY caller of the three actions, and it is used ONLY by the meta_campaigns detail page; no campaigns-table-backed page passes a lowercase-status id anywhere.

The claim's second limb is factually correct in isolation: campaigns_status_check allows only draft|scheduled|completed|cancelled (v1_baseline.sql:206, remote-v1-backup.sql:317), no migration adds 'paused' (grep found none), so an UPDATE setting status:'paused' on a real campaigns row would throw a CHECK violation. However, that path is never reached because nothing in the app passes a genuine campaigns.id with status 'scheduled' to these actions.

The correct characterisation is entirely-unwired dead code: the recurring lifecycle actions target the organic `campaigns` table but are only ever surfaced (behind a permanently-false gate) on a `meta_campaigns` page, so no user can trigger them at all. That is materially different from the claimed high-severity 'both paths always fail for every paid campaign id' production bug. Because the stated failure scenario and root cause (a live mis-targeted query returning 'Campaign not found' to users) cannot actually happen, the claim as written is rejected. The constraint mismatch is a real latent issue but does not support the asserted severity or user-facing failure mode.
